Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a consistent study routine. I'd go through phases where I'd study intensely for a few days, then completely drift away for a week or more. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found it hard to just sit and read through materials—I needed to be doing something with my hands or moving around. Most of my studying happened in bursts, usually when I felt the pressure building up or when I was in the right mood.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd tell myself I'd study every evening, but then life would get in the way—extracurriculars, hanging out with friends, or just not feeling mentally present. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced any consistency. When an exam was coming up, I'd suddenly become very focused, but otherwise, my schedule was pretty chaotic.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or that creeping anxiety telling me I'd been away too long. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, scroll through quickly, and then log out without really doing much. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often because they felt less intimidating than regular coursework.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I'd check in daily because of the gamified challenges—those points and badges actually made me curious to see my progress. But that routine never stuck for long. My anxiety would spike, I'd feel overwhelmed looking at all the tasks I hadn't completed, and then I'd avoid the platform altogether for days.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could watch them while doing other things or moving around. The gamified tasks were also engaging because they broke things down into smaller, less scary pieces. Discussion forums were okay too—I liked reading what other students thought, even if I didn't always contribute much myself.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just felt like walls of text that I couldn't engage with as a kinesthetic learner. I'd download them thinking I'd read them later, but later never came. The detailed assignment guidelines I'd also skim over, which probably explains my low assignment scores despite doing well on exams.
Q7: If something felt like it directly connected to what would be on the exam or gave me immediate feedback, I was more likely to do it. The gamified elements helped because there was instant gratification—points, progress bars, that kind of thing. Activities that felt abstract or disconnected from any tangible outcome just didn't register as worth my limited energy.
Q8: Required activities carried this weight of obligation that honestly made me anxious and more likely to procrastinate. Optional activities felt lighter, like I could explore them without consequences. Ironically, I sometimes engaged more genuinely with optional content because there was no pressure attached to it.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my phone and review them during commutes or while working out. I also used YouTube tutorials and external resources that explained concepts in more hands-on ways. A lot of my actual learning happened through practice problems I'd find online or discussions with classmates outside the platform.
Q10: Before exams, I'd spend hours with physical flashcards I made myself, pacing around my room and quizzing myself out loud. I'd also meet up with study groups where we'd work through problems on whiteboards. None of that showed up in the LMS, but that's honestly where most of my real understanding came from—doing things with my hands and talking through concepts.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video lecture once, then watch it multiple times offline over the next few days. Same with slides—I'd print them out and annotate them by hand. The LMS would show one access, but I might have spent hours with that same material in physical form.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where my anxiety got really bad and I just couldn't face the platform. Seeing all those incomplete tasks and low completion percentages made me feel like a failure, which made me avoid it more. It became this vicious cycle—the more I fell behind, the harder it was to come back.
Q13: Massively. When I had extracurricular commitments piling up or when I was just mentally exhausted, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional also made me disengage—I'd feel paralyzed trying to figure out priorities. Fatigue from high anxiety meant some days I just couldn't process anything.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be completely elsewhere—worrying about other things or just feeling too anxious to absorb anything. I'd watch videos on 2x speed just to mark them as complete without really learning anything.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around them was just too much—I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more capable. Starting felt like the hardest part, and once I'd procrastinated past a certain point, the shame made it even harder to begin.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd be completely inactive until about 48 hours before something was due, then I'd frantically try to catch up. My LMS usage would spike dramatically around due dates and flatline in between. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could push through the anxiety.
Q17: Positive feedback actually helped a lot—when I did well on an exam, I felt more motivated to engage afterward. But negative feedback or seeing low assignment scores made me want to withdraw and avoid the course. The gamified feedback was gentler somehow, showing progress without making me feel judged.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having everything organized in modules was good, but seeing how much content there was in each module triggered my anxiety. I wished things were broken into even smaller chunks. The gamified elements helped make the structure feel less intimidating.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they gave me entry points that felt achievable. But the sheer volume of content and the way assignments were weighted made things harder. When I saw that assignments were a big portion of the grade but felt too anxious to start them, I just focused on exam prep instead.
Q20: I think that was basically me. I'd log in, browse around, watch some videos, participate in discussions occasionally, but then freeze up when it came to actually submitting work. The anxiety around assignments being judged was paralyzing. Being present on the platform didn't mean I could convert that presence into completed work.
Q21: That also reflects my experience, especially with exams. I did really well on the final exam despite low LMS activity because I studied extensively offline and in ways that worked for my learning style. The LMS captured maybe 30% of my actual study time. Being a kinesthetic learner, my real learning happened away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often you accessed materials, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if someone was genuinely learning or just clicking through anxiously. They also miss all offline studying, group work, and the mental and emotional state of the student during their interactions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when you think about course concepts even outside study time, when you want to do well because the content matters to you. It's not about how many times you logged in—it's about whether your mind was truly present.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything—how I'd sometimes stare at the screen unable to click on anything, or how I'd study for hours offline because the platform itself stressed me out. It also wouldn't show that despite my low engagement metrics, I genuinely understood the material and proved it on the exam. The gap between my assignment completion and exam score tells a story that raw data can't explain.
